metadata.title: Claude, When Not.
metadata.thesis: Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.

beats.narration_text: The 'would you hide this from your teacher or boss?' test cut through everything for me, because hiding is the signal that the use is removing something that was supposed to be mine. I want to pressure-test my actual habits against it. Help me: (1) take five specific things I do with Claude this week and run each through the hide-it test, marking scaffold or crutch; (2) explain why skipping the first draft of something I'm meant to learn by drafting counts as skipping the class; (3) turn the H1-through-H5 season into one sentence I can tape to my laptop.

props.command: The 'would you hide this from your teacher or boss?' test cut through everything for me, because hiding is the signal that the use is removing something that was supposed to be mine. I want to pressure-test my actual habits against it. Help me: (1) take five specific things I do with Claude this week and run each through the hide-it test, marking scaffold or crutch; (2) explain why skipping the first draft of something I'm meant to learn by drafting counts as skipping the class; (3) turn the H1-through-H5 season into one sentence I can tape to my laptop.
output: the first draft of anything you're learning to draft
facts you won't verify, decisions you'd hide

beats.narration_text: Use it for leverage. Quizzing yourself on your own material — that is the H3 study stack. Feedback on a draft you wrote — you did the primary work. Explanations for things you are confused about. Practice generation: problems, flashcards, examples. Planning and scheduling — it is an excellent logistician. These are all scaffold uses. You are in the driver's seat.

props.heading: Use AI for leverage.
props.body:
USE for:
— quizzing yourself on your own material
— feedback on a draft YOU wrote
— explanations of things you're confused about
— practice generation (problems, flashcards)
— planning and scheduling

All scaffold uses. You're in the driver's seat.

beats.narration_text: Do not use it for: graded work without disclosure — that is the line H1 drew. The first draft of anything you are supposed to learn by drafting — the drafting is the learning, and skipping it means you skipped the class. Facts you will not verify — your name on the output means you checked it. Anything you would hide from your teacher or your boss. That last one is the test.

props.heading: Don't use AI for.
props.body:
DON'T USE for:
— graded work without disclosure
— the first draft of anything you're supposed to learn by drafting
— facts you won't verify
— anything you'd hide from your teacher or boss

The last one is the test.

beats.narration_text: The one question that collapses all of it. Would you hide this use from your teacher or your boss? If yes — do not do it. Not because you will get caught. Because the hiding means the use is removing something that was supposed to be yours. The skill, the learning, the accountability. Hiding is the signal that you know the use is a crutch, not a scaffold. The test is not about getting caught. It is about who you are becoming.

props.heading: Would you hide it?
props.body:
Would you hide this use from your teacher or boss?

If yes — don't.
Not because you'll get caught.
Because the hiding means the use is removing something
that was supposed to be yours.

The test is about who you're becoming.

beats.narration_text: Commit before the reveal. Which of these is a do not use? A: ask Claude to quiz you on a chapter. B: ask Claude to write your essay. C: ask Claude to explain a confusing concept. D: ask Claude to give feedback on your draft. One of these removes the learning that is supposed to be yours. Pick it before the next beat.

props.command: which is a don't-use? A) quiz you B) write your essay C) explain a concept D) give feedback on your draft
props.runningText: commit to A, B, C, or D…

beats.narration_text: B. Writing your essay removes the drafting — which was the skill you were supposed to build. A, C, and D are all scaffold uses: quizzing, explaining, giving feedback. The rest of the season laid this out. H1: ask permission, know the line. H2: quiz do not answer — the trade. H3: the quizmaster study stack. H4: conduct, do not compete. H5 is this card. Five pieces, one principle: use it for leverage — not in place of the thing you are supposed to learn.

props.heading: The season's five pieces.
props.body:
H1: ask permission · know the line
H2: quiz don't answer · the trade
H3: quizmaster stack
H4: daily audit loop
H5: when not (this card)

One principle: use it for leverage — not in place of
the thing you're supposed to learn.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The when and not card. Use it for leverage — quizzing, feedback, explanations, practice, planning. Do not use it for drafts you are supposed to write, graded work without disclosure, unverified facts, or anything you would hide. The one question: would you hide this? The thesis: knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill. That is your card.

artifactLines: USE for: quizzing yourself, feedback on YOUR draft, explanations of confusing concepts, practice generation, planning.
DON'T USE for: graded work without disclosure, first drafts you're supposed to learn by drafting, unverified facts, anything you'd hide.
The one question: would you hide this use from your teacher or boss? If yes — don't. The hiding means the use is taking something that was supposed to be yours.
Knowing when not to use it IS the AI skill.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt into Claude and it will print you a wallet-card version of the when and not line. One thing you can keep. Replace the bracket with your actual context.

props.command: Give me a short wallet-card version of when I should and shouldn't use AI as a [student / mid-career professional]. Max 6 bullet points total. Practical and direct.
